one_line_takeaway: By jointly training a lightweight logits-perturbation encoder and neural decoder end-to-end -- using an LLM-as-differentiable-surrogate 'online prompting' trick to backpropagate through otherwise non-differentiable text editing and semantic-similarity computation -- this ICML 2025 paper achieves a reported 17.3% average robustness gain over the distortion-free DiPmark baseline (37-39% under paraphrasing specifically) while keeping perplexity and downstream task quality close to unwatermarked LLM output.
key_finding: On Table 2 (main results across OPT-1.3B/Llama2-7B/Qwen2.5-7B), the proposed method achieves an average F1 of 0.975 across clean/SS/CP/PA conditions, described as an average robustness improvement of 17.3% over DiPmark with gains of 37-39% specifically under paraphrasing (PA), and a 1.5% average gain over Unigram (peaking at +12% for copy-paste on Llama2-7B), while reducing PPL by over 9% on average versus Unigram. In the expanded Appendix Table 6 (Llama2-7B only, adding SWEET/TSW), the method reaches average F1 0.969, 3.9% above the next-best (Unigram, 0.933), with PA F1=0.916 and CP F1=0.978 (13.2% and 3.5% above Unigram on those two conditions respectively), PPL=7.730 (16.7% lower than Unigram's 9.275), BLEU=31.062 on machine translation (7.3% above the next-best, Unbiased at 28.949, and above the unwatermarked NWM baseline of 31.789 is not exceeded but is closely approached), and pass@1=34.0 on code generation (versus DiPmark/Unbiased at 36.0 and an unwatermarked ceiling of 43.0). Cross-LLM zero-shot transfer (Table 3) yields F1>=0.99 on clean text and average F1 0.969 under edits across Mixtral-7B/Llama3-8B/Llama3.2-3B, with PPL roughly 1.2x the unwatermarked baseline. Ablations (Table 5, Llama2-7B) show removing the semantic loss increases PPL by 23.6% while retaining robustness, and removing the online text editor N drops F1 by 5.7% (paraphrasing) and 2.7% (copy-paste), directly isolating the two novel training components' contributions.
core_contribution: The paper proposes an end-to-end trained logits-based LLM watermarking scheme in which a lightweight encoder E (an MLP operating on top-k candidate token sequences) and a lightweight decoder D (an LSTM + MLP classifier) are jointly optimized, rather than the encoder and decoder being trained/derived separately as in prior logits-based methods. The encoder perturbs the top-k logits at each generation step (the remainder is left at 0, forming an implicit 'grey list'); Gumbel-Softmax sampling (GSS) makes the token-sampling step differentiable so gradients from the decoder's detection loss can update the encoder end-to-end. To make two other otherwise non-differentiable pipeline stages trainable, the authors introduce an 'online prompting' technique that uses the same on-the-fly LLM M as a differentiable surrogate for (a) simulating user edits of watermarked text (by prompting M to rewrite the text, replacing prior random token drop/add style augmentation) and (b) computing a semantic-preservation loss (by prompting M with an in-context example to produce a one-word 'semantic embedding' summary, then taking cosine distance between watermarked and non-watermarked outputs, avoiding an external embedding model with a mismatched tokenizer). The system is trained on one LLM (OPT-1.3B) and generalizes to other LLMs at inference time via a 'converter' module that maps a target LLM's tokenization/embeddings into the space the encoder/decoder were trained on, exploiting the empirical finding (Table 1, normalized Levenshtein token-alignment similarity across OPT-1.3B/Llama2-7B/NLLB-600M/BERT-base tokenizers, average 73.4%) that different tokenizers segment the same sentences similarly enough for this to work without retraining.
threat_model: The setting is provenance/authorship attribution and copyright/misuse detection for LLM-generated text: a text-generation service embeds a statistical watermark in its outputs so that a verifier (holding the trained decoder) can later determine, from a given piece of text, whether it originated from the watermarked model, even after the text has undergone adversarial or incidental modification. The adversary is a 'downstream editor' of the text (not an attacker targeting the model's weights or training data) who applies transformations intended to strip or degrade the watermark signal without the verifier's knowledge; the paper tests three such transformations: synonymous substitution (SS), copy-paste attack (CP, i.e., mixing watermarked and non-watermarked spans), and paraphrasing (PA, via LLM-driven rewriting). Watermark theft/spoofing is mentioned only as an appendix topic (Sec. B.3, not covered by the extracted pages) and is not the paper's primary threat model. The defender's goal is to maximize detection F1 under these edits while keeping generated-text quality (perplexity and downstream task performance) close to unwatermarked output.
limitations: The paper's own text notes: (1) increasing k from 20 to 40 raises log-diversity but also increases perplexity, and delta trades off robustness against quality (lower delta=0.75 gives best PPL 7.057 but weaker paraphrase F1=0.854; higher delta=1.5 gives best paraphrase F1=0.962 but 21.3% higher PPL than delta=0.75) -- so the reported headline numbers depend on a manually chosen operating point (delta=1.25, k=20) rather than a dominant configuration. (2) The cross-LLM converter relies on an empirical token-alignment similarity of only 73.4% (Table 1) between tokenizer pairs, and the paper itself flags that token merging/splitting during conversion (cases 1 and 2 in Sec. 3.6) 'may negatively impact performance of the watermark model,' with only a probabilistic argument (0.7% chance of majority-candidate misalignment at k=20) offered as reassurance rather than a guarantee. (3) The method sacrifices the closed-form statistical guarantees (p-values) of KGW-style analytical watermarks in favor of a learned neural decoder's scalar confidence score; the paper frames this as offering 'guarantees analogous to' provable p-values via a red/green-list retrieval reconstruction, but this is a post hoc interpretability aid rather than a formal detection guarantee. (4) Code generation quality (pass@1=34.0) trails the distortion-free baselines DiPmark and Unbiased (both 36.0), an acknowledged case where the method does not lead. (5) The paper does not evaluate against a watermark remover/adversary with white-box knowledge of the encoder/decoder architecture, or against training-time attacks; robustness is only tested against generic text-editing operations, not against an adversary specifically targeting this watermarking scheme.

datasets_used: C4 (evaluation prompts, 30-token prefixes, 200 generated tokens per sample)
WMT16 German-English (machine translation downstream task, with NLLB-600M)
HumanEval (code generation downstream task, with Starcoder)
quantitative_results: Main Table 2 (OPT-1.3B/Llama2-7B/Qwen2.5-7B avg): average F1 0.975 across CL/SS/CP/PA; +17.3% average robustness over DiPmark; +37-39% F1 under paraphrasing vs DiPmark; +1.5% average over Unigram, peaking +12% on copy-paste for Llama2-7B; >9% average PPL reduction vs Unigram

Appendix Table 6 (Llama2-7B, extended competitor set): average F1 0.969 vs Unigram's 0.933 (+3.9%); PA F1 0.916, CP F1 0.978 (+3.5%, +13.2% over Unigram); PPL 7.730 vs Unigram's 9.275 (-16.7%); BLEU 31.062 (vs Unbiased 28.949, +7.3%; vs NWM ceiling 31.789); pass@1 34.0 (vs DiPmark/Unbiased 36.0, vs NWM ceiling 43.0, vs Unigram's 33.0)
Table 3 (cross-LLM zero-shot, Mixtral-7B/Llama3-8B/Llama3.2-3B): clean F1 >=0.99 on all three; average edited F1 0.969; PPL approx. 1.2x the no-watermark baseline
Fig. 5 operating-point comparison at PPL threshold 8 (NWM PPL=6.8): +18.16% robustness under paraphrasing and +20.42% under copy-paste vs second-best method Unigram

Table 5 ablation (Llama2-7B): removing semantic loss increases PPL by 23.6%; removing online editor N drops F1 by 5.7% (paraphrasing) and 2.7% (copy-paste); delta=0.75 gives PPL=7.057 but PA F1=0.854; delta=1.5 gives PA F1=0.962, CP F1=0.992 but 21.3% higher PPL than delta=0.75; chosen operating point delta=1.25,k=20 gives PA F1=0.916, PPL=7.730
Table 7 (sampling strategy, Llama2-7B): under multinomial sampling, method beats Unigram by 3.5% (PA) and 13.2% (CP); under beam search (5 beams), method beats KGW by 2.3% (PA) and 2.1% (CP)
baselines_compared: SWEET (Lee et al., 2023) - Appendix A.1 only
TSW (Huo et al., 2024) - Appendix A.1 only
